sourceRefs.fallbackText: This module provides regular expression matching operations similar to those found in Perl.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This module provides regular expression matching operations similar to those found in Perl.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Both patterns and strings to be searched can be Unicode strings ( str ) as well as 8-bit strings ( bytes ). However, Unicode strings and 8-bit strings cannot be mixed: that is, you cannot match a Unicode string with a bytes pattern or vice-versa; similarly, when asking for a substitution, the replacement string must be of the same type as both the pattern an
sourceRefs.textQuote: Both patterns and strings to be searched can be Unicode strings ( str ) as well as 8-bit strings ( bytes ). However, Unicode strings and 8-bit strings cannot be mixed: that is, you cannot match a Unicode string with a by

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Regular expressions use the backslash character ( '\' ) to indicate special forms or to allow special characters to be used without invoking their special meaning. This collides with Python’s usage of the same character for the same purpose in string literals; for example, to match a literal backslash, one might have to write '\\\\' as the pattern string, be
sourceRefs.textQuote: Regular expressions use the backslash character ( '\' ) to indicate special forms or to allow special characters to be used without invoking their special meaning. This collides with Python’s usage of the same character

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The solution is to use Python’s raw string notation for regular expression patterns; backslashes are not handled in any special way in a string literal prefixed with 'r' . So r"\n" is a two-character string containing '\' and 'n' , while "\n" is a one-character string containing a newline. Usually patterns will be expressed in Python code using this raw stri
sourceRefs.textQuote: The solution is to use Python’s raw string notation for regular expression patterns; backslashes are not handled in any special way in a string literal prefixed with 'r' . So r"\n" is a two-character string containing '\

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is important to note that most regular expression operations are available as module-level functions and methods on compiled regular expressions . The functions are shortcuts that don’t require you to compile a regex object first, but miss some fine-tuning parameters.
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is important to note that most regular expression operations are available as module-level functions and methods on compiled regular expressions . The functions are shortcuts that don’t require you to compile a regex

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The third-party regex module, which has an API compatible with the standard library re module, but offers additional functionality and a more thorough Unicode support.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The third-party regex module, which has an API compatible with the standard library re module, but offers additional functionality and a more thorough Unicode support.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A regular expression (or RE) specifies a set of strings that matches it; the functions in this module let you check if a particular string matches a given regular expression (or if a given regular expression matches a particular string, which comes down to the same thing).
sourceRefs.textQuote: A regular expression (or RE) specifies a set of strings that matches it; the functions in this module let you check if a particular string matches a given regular expression (or if a given regular expression matches a pa

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Regular expressions can be concatenated to form new regular expressions; if A and B are both regular expressions, then AB is also a regular expression. In general, if a string p matches A and another string q matches B , the string pq will match AB. This holds unless A or B contain low precedence operations; boundary conditions between A and B ; or have numb
sourceRefs.textQuote: Regular expressions can be concatenated to form new regular expressions; if A and B are both regular expressions, then AB is also a regular expression. In general, if a string p matches A and another string q matches B ,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A brief explanation of the format of regular expressions follows. For further information and a gentler presentation, consult the Regular expression HOWTO .
sourceRefs.textQuote: A brief explanation of the format of regular expressions follows. For further information and a gentler presentation, consult the Regular expression HOWTO .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Regular expressions can contain both special and ordinary characters. Most ordinary characters, like 'A' , 'a' , or '0' , are the simplest regular expressions; they simply match themselves. You can concatenate ordinary characters, so last matches the string 'last' . (In the rest of this section, we’ll write RE’s in this special style , usually without quotes
sourceRefs.textQuote: Regular expressions can contain both special and ordinary characters. Most ordinary characters, like 'A' , 'a' , or '0' , are the simplest regular expressions; they simply match themselves. You can concatenate ordinary c

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Some characters, like '|' or '(' , are special. Special characters either stand for classes of ordinary characters, or affect how the regular expressions around them are interpreted.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Some characters, like '|' or '(' , are special. Special characters either stand for classes of ordinary characters, or affect how the regular expressions around them are interpreted.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Repetition operators or quantifiers ( * , + , ? , {m,n} , etc) cannot be directly nested. This avoids ambiguity with the non-greedy modifier suffix ? , and with other modifiers in other implementations. To apply a second repetition to an inner repetition, parentheses may be used. For example, the expression (?:a{6})* matches any multiple of six 'a' character
sourceRefs.textQuote: Repetition operators or quantifiers ( * , + , ? , {m,n} , etc) cannot be directly nested. This avoids ambiguity with the non-greedy modifier suffix ? , and with other modifiers in other implementations. To apply a second

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The special characters are:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The special characters are:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (Dot.) In the default mode, this matches any character except a newline. If the DOTALL flag has been specified, this matches any character including a newline. (?s:.) matches any character regardless of flags.
sourceRefs.textQuote: (Dot.) In the default mode, this matches any character except a newline. If the DOTALL flag has been specified, this matches any character including a newline. (?s:.) matches any character regardless of flags.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (Caret.) Matches the start of the string, and in MULTILINE mode also matches immediately after each newline.
sourceRefs.textQuote: (Caret.) Matches the start of the string, and in MULTILINE mode also matches immediately after each newline.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches the end of the string or just before the newline at the end of the string, and in MULTILINE mode also matches before a newline. foo matches both ‘foo’ and ‘foobar’, while the regular expression foo$ matches only ‘foo’. More interestingly, searching for foo.$ in 'foo1\nfoo2\n' matches ‘foo2’ normally, but ‘foo1’ in MULTILINE mode; searching for a sing
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches the end of the string or just before the newline at the end of the string, and in MULTILINE mode also matches before a newline. foo matches both ‘foo’ and ‘foobar’, while the regular expression foo$ matches only

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Causes the resulting RE to match 0 or more repetitions of the preceding RE, as many repetitions as are possible. ab* will match ‘a’, ‘ab’, or ‘a’ followed by any number of ‘b’s.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Causes the resulting RE to match 0 or more repetitions of the preceding RE, as many repetitions as are possible. ab* will match ‘a’, ‘ab’, or ‘a’ followed by any number of ‘b’s.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Causes the resulting RE to match 1 or more repetitions of the preceding RE. ab+ will match ‘a’ followed by any non-zero number of ‘b’s; it will not match just ‘a’.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Causes the resulting RE to match 1 or more repetitions of the preceding RE. ab+ will match ‘a’ followed by any non-zero number of ‘b’s; it will not match just ‘a’.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Causes the resulting RE to match 0 or 1 repetitions of the preceding RE. ab? will match either ‘a’ or ‘ab’.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Causes the resulting RE to match 0 or 1 repetitions of the preceding RE. ab? will match either ‘a’ or ‘ab’.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The '*' , '+' , and '?' quantifiers are all greedy ; they match as much text as possible. Sometimes this behaviour isn’t desired; if the RE <.*> is matched against '<a> b <c>' , it will match the entire string, and not just '<a>' . Adding ? after the quantifier makes it perform the match in non-greedy or minimal fashion; as few characters as possible will be
sourceRefs.textQuote: The '*' , '+' , and '?' quantifiers are all greedy ; they match as much text as possible. Sometimes this behaviour isn’t desired; if the RE <.*> is matched against '<a> b <c>' , it will match the entire string, and not j

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Like the '*' , '+' , and '?' quantifiers, those where '+' is appended also match as many times as possible. However, unlike the true greedy quantifiers, these do not allow back-tracking when the expression following it fails to match. These are known as possessive quantifiers. For example, a*a will match 'aaaa' because the a* will match all 4 'a' s, but, whe

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Added in version 3.11.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Added in version 3.11.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Specifies that exactly m copies of the previous RE should be matched; fewer matches cause the entire RE not to match. For example, a{6} will match exactly six 'a' characters, but not five.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Specifies that exactly m copies of the previous RE should be matched; fewer matches cause the entire RE not to match. For example, a{6} will match exactly six 'a' characters, but not five.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Causes the resulting RE to match from m to n repetitions of the preceding RE, attempting to match as many repetitions as possible. For example, a{3,5} will match from 3 to 5 'a' characters. Omitting m specifies a lower bound of zero, and omitting n specifies an infinite upper bound. As an example, a{4,}b will match 'aaaab' or a thousand 'a' characters follow
sourceRefs.textQuote: Causes the resulting RE to match from m to n repetitions of the preceding RE, attempting to match as many repetitions as possible. For example, a{3,5} will match from 3 to 5 'a' characters. Omitting m specifies a lower b

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Causes the resulting RE to match from m to n repetitions of the preceding RE, attempting to match as few repetitions as possible. This is the non-greedy version of the previous quantifier. For example, on the 6-character string 'aaaaaa' , a{3,5} will match 5 'a' characters, while a{3,5}? will only match 3 characters.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Causes the resulting RE to match from m to n repetitions of the preceding RE, attempting to match as few repetitions as possible. This is the non-greedy version of the previous quantifier. For example, on the 6-character

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Causes the resulting RE to match from m to n repetitions of the preceding RE, attempting to match as many repetitions as possible without establishing any backtracking points. This is the possessive version of the quantifier above. For example, on the 6-character string 'aaaaaa' , a{3,5}+aa attempt to match 5 'a' characters, then, requiring 2 more 'a' s, wil
sourceRefs.textQuote: Causes the resulting RE to match from m to n repetitions of the preceding RE, attempting to match as many repetitions as possible without establishing any backtracking points. This is the possessive version of the quanti

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Added in version 3.11.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Added in version 3.11.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Either escapes special characters (permitting you to match characters like '*' , '?' , and so forth), or signals a special sequence; special sequences are discussed below.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Either escapes special characters (permitting you to match characters like '*' , '?' , and so forth), or signals a special sequence; special sequences are discussed below.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Characters can be listed individually, e.g. [amk] will match 'a' , 'm' , or 'k' .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Characters can be listed individually, e.g. [amk] will match 'a' , 'm' , or 'k' .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Ranges of characters can be indicated by giving two characters and separating them by a '-' , for example [a-z] will match any lowercase ASCII letter, [0-5][0-9] will match all the two-digits numbers from 00 to 59 , and [0-9A-Fa-f] will match any hexadecimal digit. If - is escaped (e.g. [a\-z] ) or if it’s placed as the first or last character (e.g. [-a] or
sourceRefs.textQuote: Ranges of characters can be indicated by giving two characters and separating them by a '-' , for example [a-z] will match any lowercase ASCII letter, [0-5][0-9] will match all the two-digits numbers from 00 to 59 , and

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Special characters except backslash lose their special meaning inside sets. For example, [(+*)] will match any of the literal characters '(' , '+' , '*' , or ')' .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Special characters except backslash lose their special meaning inside sets. For example, [(+*)] will match any of the literal characters '(' , '+' , '*' , or ')' .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Backslash either escapes characters which have special meaning in a set such as '-' , ']' , '^' and '\\' itself or signals a special sequence which represents a single character such as \xa0 or \n or a character class such as \w or \S (defined below). Note that \b represents a single “backspace” character, not a word boundary as outside a set, and numeric es
sourceRefs.textQuote: Backslash either escapes characters which have special meaning in a set such as '-' , ']' , '^' and '\\' itself or signals a special sequence which represents a single character such as \xa0 or \n or a character class su

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Characters that are not within a range can be matched by complementing the set. If the first character of the set is '^' , all the characters that are not in the set will be matched. For example, [^5] will match any character except '5' , and [^^] will match any character except '^' . ^ has no special meaning if it’s not the first character in the set.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Characters that are not within a range can be matched by complementing the set. If the first character of the set is '^' , all the characters that are not in the set will be matched. For example, [^5] will match any char

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To match a literal ']' inside a set, precede it with a backslash, or place it at the beginning of the set. For example, both [()[\]{}] and []()[{}] will match a right bracket, as well as left bracket, braces, and parentheses.
sourceRefs.textQuote: To match a literal ']' inside a set, precede it with a backslash, or place it at the beginning of the set. For example, both [()[\]{}] and []()[{}] will match a right bracket, as well as left bracket, braces, and parenth

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Support of nested sets and set operations as in Unicode Technical Standard #18 might be added in the future. sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.7: FutureWarning is raised if a character set contains constructs that will change semantically in the future.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A|B , where A and B can be arbitrary REs, creates a regular expression that will match either A or B . An arbitrary number of REs can be separated by the '|' in this way. sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches if ... matches next, but doesn’t consume any of the string. This is called a lookahead assertion . For example, Isaac (?=Asimov) will match 'Isaac ' only if it’s followed by 'Asimov' .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches if ... matches next, but doesn’t consume any of the string. This is called a lookahead assertion . For example, Isaac (?=Asimov) will match 'Isaac ' only if it’s followed by 'Asimov' .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches if ... doesn’t match next. This is a negative lookahead assertion . For example, Isaac (?!Asimov) will match 'Isaac ' only if it’s not followed by 'Asimov' .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches if ... doesn’t match next. This is a negative lookahead assertion . For example, Isaac (?!Asimov) will match 'Isaac ' only if it’s not followed by 'Asimov' .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches if the current position in the string is preceded by a match for ... that ends at the current position. This is called a positive lookbehind assertion . sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> import re >>> m = re . search ( '(?<=abc)def' , 'abcdef' ) >>> m . group ( 0 ) 'def'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> import re >>> m = re . search ( '(?<=abc)def' , 'abcdef' ) >>> m . group ( 0 ) 'def'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This example looks for a word following a hyphen:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This example looks for a word following a hyphen:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> m = re . search ( r '(?<=-)\w+' , 'spam-egg' ) >>> m . group ( 0 ) 'egg'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> m = re . search ( r '(?<=-)\w+' , 'spam-egg' ) >>> m . group ( 0 ) 'egg'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.5: Added support for group references of fixed length.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.5: Added support for group references of fixed length.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches if the current position in the string is not preceded by a match for ... . This is called a negative lookbehind assertion . Similar to positive lookbehind assertions, the contained pattern must only match strings of some fixed length. Patterns which start with negative lookbehind assertions may match at the beginning of the string being searched.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches if the current position in the string is not preceded by a match for ... . This is called a negative lookbehind assertion . Similar to positive lookbehind assertions, the contained pattern must only match strings

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Will try to match with yes-pattern if the group with given id or name exists, and with no-pattern if it doesn’t. no-pattern is optional and can be omitted. For example, (<)?(\w+@\w+(?:\.\w+)+)(?(1)>|$) is a poor email matching pattern, which will match with '<user@host.com>' as well as 'user@host.com' , but not with '<user@host.com' nor 'user@host.com>' .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Will try to match with yes-pattern if the group with given id or name exists, and with no-pattern if it doesn’t. no-pattern is optional and can be omitted. For example, (<)?(\w+@\w+(?:\.\w+)+)(?(1)>|$) is a poor email ma

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.12: Group id can only contain ASCII digits. In bytes patterns, group name can only contain bytes in the ASCII range ( b'\x00' - b'\x7f' ).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.12: Group id can only contain ASCII digits. In bytes patterns, group name can only contain bytes in the ASCII range ( b'\x00' - b'\x7f' ).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The special sequences consist of '\' and a character from the list below. If the ordinary character is not an ASCII digit or an ASCII letter, then the resulting RE will match the second character. For example, \$ matches the character '$' .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches the contents of the group of the same number. Groups are numbered starting from 1. For example, (.+) \1 matches 'the the' or '55 55' , but not 'thethe' (note the space after the group). This special sequence can only be used to match one of the first 99 groups. If the first digit of number is 0, or number is 3 octal digits long, it will not be interp
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches the contents of the group of the same number. Groups are numbered starting from 1. For example, (.+) \1 matches 'the the' or '55 55' , but not 'thethe' (note the space after the group). This special sequence can

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches only at the start of the string.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches only at the start of the string.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches the empty string, but only at the beginning or end of a word. A word is defined as a sequence of word characters. Note that formally, \b is defined as the boundary between a \w and a \W character (or vice versa), or between \w and the beginning or end of the string. This means that r'\bat\b' matches 'at' , 'at.' , '(at)' , and 'as at ay' but not 'att
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches the empty string, but only at the beginning or end of a word. A word is defined as a sequence of word characters. Note that formally, \b is defined as the boundary between a \w and a \W character (or vice versa),

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The default word characters in Unicode (str) patterns are Unicode alphanumerics and the underscore, but this can be changed by using the ASCII flag. Word boundaries are determined by the current locale if the LOCALE flag is used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The default word characters in Unicode (str) patterns are Unicode alphanumerics and the underscore, but this can be changed by using the ASCII flag. Word boundaries are determined by the current locale if the LOCALE flag

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Inside a character range, \b represents the backspace character, for compatibility with Python’s string literals.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Inside a character range, \b represents the backspace character, for compatibility with Python’s string literals.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches the empty string, but only when it is not at the beginning or end of a word. This means that r'at\B' matches 'athens' , 'atom' , 'attorney' , but not 'at' , 'at.' , or 'at!' . \B is the opposite of \b , so word characters in Unicode (str) patterns are Unicode alphanumerics or the underscore, although this can be changed by using the ASCII flag. sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.14: \B now matches empty input string.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.14: \B now matches empty input string.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches any Unicode decimal digit (that is, any character in Unicode character category [Nd] ). This includes [0-9] , and also many other digit characters.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches any Unicode decimal digit (that is, any character in Unicode character category [Nd] ). This includes [0-9] , and also many other digit characters.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches [0-9] if the ASCII flag is used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches [0-9] if the ASCII flag is used.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches any decimal digit in the ASCII character set; this is equivalent to [0-9] .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches any decimal digit in the ASCII character set; this is equivalent to [0-9] .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches any character which is not a decimal digit. This is the opposite of \d .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches any character which is not a decimal digit. This is the opposite of \d .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches [^0-9] if the ASCII flag is used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches [^0-9] if the ASCII flag is used.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches Unicode whitespace characters (as defined by str.isspace() ). This includes [ \t\n\r\f\v] , and also many other characters, for example the non-breaking spaces mandated by typography rules in many languages.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches Unicode whitespace characters (as defined by str.isspace() ). This includes [ \t\n\r\f\v] , and also many other characters, for example the non-breaking spaces mandated by typography rules in many languages.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches [ \t\n\r\f\v] if the ASCII flag is used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches [ \t\n\r\f\v] if the ASCII flag is used.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches characters considered whitespace in the ASCII character set; this is equivalent to [ \t\n\r\f\v] .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches characters considered whitespace in the ASCII character set; this is equivalent to [ \t\n\r\f\v] .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches any character which is not a whitespace character. This is the opposite of \s .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches any character which is not a whitespace character. This is the opposite of \s .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches [^ \t\n\r\f\v] if the ASCII flag is used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches [^ \t\n\r\f\v] if the ASCII flag is used.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches Unicode word characters; this includes all Unicode alphanumeric characters (as defined by str.isalnum() ), as well as the underscore ( _ ).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches Unicode word characters; this includes all Unicode alphanumeric characters (as defined by str.isalnum() ), as well as the underscore ( _ ).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches [a-zA-Z0-9_] if the ASCII flag is used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches [a-zA-Z0-9_] if the ASCII flag is used.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches characters considered alphanumeric in the ASCII character set; this is equivalent to [a-zA-Z0-9_] . If the LOCALE flag is used, matches characters considered alphanumeric in the current locale and the underscore.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches characters considered alphanumeric in the ASCII character set; this is equivalent to [a-zA-Z0-9_] . If the LOCALE flag is used, matches characters considered alphanumeric in the current locale and the underscore.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches any character which is not a word character. This is the opposite of \w . By default, matches non-underscore ( _ ) characters for which str.isalnum() returns False .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches any character which is not a word character. This is the opposite of \w . By default, matches non-underscore ( _ ) characters for which str.isalnum() returns False .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches [^a-zA-Z0-9_] if the ASCII flag is used.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches [^a-zA-Z0-9_] if the ASCII flag is used.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the LOCALE flag is used, matches characters which are neither alphanumeric in the current locale nor the underscore.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If the LOCALE flag is used, matches characters which are neither alphanumeric in the current locale nor the underscore.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Matches only at the end of the string.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Matches only at the end of the string.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Added in version 3.14.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Added in version 3.14.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The same as \z . For compatibility with old Python versions.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The same as \z . For compatibility with old Python versions.
